patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch bypasses swap readahead for zswap, an in-memory compression backend similar to zram. The current implementation of swap readahead is optimized for traditional disk-based swap devices and can cause issues like inflated anon residency, page cache thrashing, and increased fault latency when applied to an in-memory backend. By skipping readahead for zswap, the patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary prefetching of pages that are not needed. The results from testing this bypass show significant improvements in build time, swap-in/out rates, and reduced swap readahead hits.

entries (covered by the readahead window) will also be in zswap,

>> When dumpping a tree block, btrfs_header::owner is printed as
>> unsigned, which can result in numbers that are hard to read, e.g:

>>   BTRFS info (device loop0): leaf 8908800 gen 16 total ptrs 28 free space 1676 owner 18446744073709551607

>> For the above output, 18446744073709551607 is (s64)-9, the root id of data
>> reloc tree.
>>
>> Despite those pre-defined root ids that are already negative, existing
>> subvolume trees will not have any negative values, as subvolume trees can
>> only utilize the lower 48 bits, so there will be no output change for
>> existing subvolumes, thus no extra confusion.

> What do you think about a macro or function wrapping a switch statement
> that also prints the string name of known trees?

That is possible, but I'm not sure if we need to go this complex for 
kernel modules.

A signed s64 output would be enough for most developers/advanced users 
to debug.
For end users, I do not know if things like 2 or EXTENT_TREE make any 
difference.

> What about some identifying
> info like a volume path for subvol trees? That one might be a stretch.

That's is too dangerous, it will need extra tree search and backref lookup.
Meanwhile for a tree dump inside a kernel, it's already an emergency, 
normally we do not have the extra headroom for such complex workload 
just to make the subvolume id a little easier to read.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `memory_block_aligned_range()` to the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function takes an input range and aligns its start address up and end address down to memory block boundaries, which is required for memory hotplug operations. This helper is added as a common function in `<linux/memory.h>` and is used by the `dax/kmem` driver to ensure that memory ranges are properly aligned. The patch also updates the `dax/kmem` code to use this new helper function instead of manually aligning the range. The goal of this patch is to simplify the process of handling memory hotplug operations and reduce the risk of errors due to misaligned ranges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block()` function to accept an online type through its argument, rather than using a default value. This change allows callers to specify explicit online types and prepares for future modifications.

review_comments.author: Gupta, Pankaj
review_comments.summary: Pankaj Gupta reviewed the patch and gave it a Reviewed-by tag without providing any specific feedback or concerns.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to use it even when hotplug is disabled. The function returns a default online type of `MMOP_OFFLINE`, which is non-destructive and suitable for DAX use cases. This change enables drivers like CXL to build and use DAX without requiring hotplug as a predicate. The patch also adds an inline implementation for the function in case it's not enabled by CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new interface to the Linux kernel's memory hotplug functionality, allowing drivers to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver is the primary motivator for this change, as it needs to plumb a preferred policy through the dax kmem driver. The patch refactors existing APIs and adds new helpers to support this feature, while ensuring that only known in-tree modules can override the system-wide onlining policy.

review_comments.author: Gupta, Pankaj
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() that allows callers to manage multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation under a single lock. The function takes an array of ranges and processes them in two phases: first, it offlines every block of every range, and then it removes the ranges only if all ranges are offline. If any offline fails, the whole operation is reverted, providing callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step. This resolves a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource by giving them all-or-nothing semantics. The existing offline_and_remove_memory() function becomes a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows drivers that use the dax_kmem driver to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. It adds a new field, 'online_type', to the DAX device creation path, which can be used to override the system default online policy. The system default is still used if no explicit policy is specified, and this change does not introduce any behavioral changes for existing callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting helper functions to manage memory hotplug operations. The new helpers, dax_kmem_init_resources, dax_kmem_cleanup_resources, dax_kmem_do_hotplug, and dax_kmem_do_hotremove, enable future extensions for more granular control over memory hotplug. This refactoring is necessary to support hot-add functionality by separating resource management from hotunplug/cleanup operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to enable atomic whole-device hotplug operations for DAX kmem devices. It introduces a new 'state' attribute that allows devices to be transitioned between different states, including unplugged, online, and offline. The patch also includes changes to prevent deadlock during unbind operations and to handle legacy auto_online_blocks=offline policy. The atomic whole-device hotplug operation is implemented using the dax_kmem_do_hotremove() function, which collects all added ranges and offlines/removes them in one operation. This patch addresses a race condition that existed when trying to offline and remove an entire multi-block DAX kmem device.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to the Linux kernel's dax subsystem, specifically targeting the kmem hotplug sysfs interface. The test checks that the 'state' attribute of a kmem-backed dax device transitions correctly between unplugged, online, and online_movable states. It also verifies that force-unbinding does not deadlock and that invalid input is rejected. Additionally, it tests a specific regression scenario where re-onlineing after unplug must re-reserve per-range resources to ensure memory removal.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control to the dax kmem driver via a sysfs 'state' attribute, allowing for transitions across entire regions of memory to be performed atomically and safely. The current implementation has a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which this patch aims to solve by introducing a new ABI with state names mirroring per-block memoryX/state attributes. The series also includes mm preparation changes to support the new hotplug control, including adding helpers for online type management and atomic offline+remove of multiple ranges. The dax/kmem feature is updated to plumb online_type through its device creation path and extract hotplug/hotremove into helper functions. A selftest is added to regression test the new attribute.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] nfsd: inter-server copy fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] nfsd: fix cpntf publish race in nfs4_init_cp_state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the NFSv4 server (nfsd) that occurs when publishing a copy notify state. The issue arises from the fact that the publication and linkage of the state to the cp_list are split across two separate critical sections, leaving a window where the entry is reachable but not yet linked. A concurrent request can exploit this window, leading to a NULL dereference and server crash. To fix this, the patch folds the cs_type assignment and list_add into the same critical section as idr_alloc_cyclic, initializes cp_list with INIT_LIST_HEAD after allocation, and switches _free_cpntf_state_locked() to use list_del_init().

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] nfsd: fix UAF in cpntf statelist drain
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS server's statelist drain function, nfs4_free_cpntf_statelist(). The bug occurs when a concurrent holder increments the reference count of a state while another thread is draining the list. The fix involves rewriting the drain to unconditionally revoke each entry before dropping its parent-owned reference, ensuring that all entries are properly removed from the list and freed in one pass.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] nfsd: fix UAF in async copy cancel and shutdown
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS daemon's async copy cancel and shutdown functionality. The issue arises when an async copy is canceled or shut down, causing the reaper to move the copy off the async_copies list while another thread tries to access it, leading to a double free of the filecache object. To fix this, the patch modifies find_async_copy() to clear cp_clp and remove the copy from async_copies after incrementing its reference count, ensuring that the reaper can no longer observe the copy. Additionally, nfsd4_stop_copy() is modified to release the list-membership reference held by the reaper before calling cleanup_async_copy().

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes bugs in the inter-server copy offload code of the NFS daemon, specifically addressing a publish race condition and use-after-free issues in the cpntf state management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] iomap: add helper to mark folio uptodate
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function, `iomap_folio_mark_uptodate`, which marks a folio as uptodate and updates its uptodate bitmap if it has iomap state data attached. This is necessary for filesystems like fuse that need to mark a folio as uptodate outside of conventional iomap calls. The function simply calls the existing `iomap_set_range_uptodate` function with the entire folio range, making it an exported and reusable solution.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] fuse: use iomap helper to mark folio uptodate
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the FUSE file system to use the iomap helper for marking folios uptodate, ensuring that both the folio-level flag and the internal bitmap are updated correctly when dealing with large folios backed by iomap. The changes affect two files: fs/fuse/file.c and fs/fuse/notify.c.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: don't clear folio uptodate on writethrough errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: don't clear folio uptodate on writethrough errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch prevents the clearing of a folio's uptodate flag in case of write-through errors on FUSE filesystems. This is necessary to maintain the invariant that non-uptodate folios must not be visible to userspace, which could lead to potential information leaks. The change ensures that if an application writes data and the server fails to persist it or only services a short write, the page cache folio will retain the original data instead of reverting to the server's contents on subsequent reads.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: enable large folios
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch enables large folios for the FUSE file system by capping the maximum order at the largest request that can be issued to a fuse device, ensuring that each folio fits within a single request. This change allows the allocator to fall back to smaller folios under memory pressure. The patch includes benchmark results showing significant performance improvements in various workloads, including sequential reads and writes, with some cases seeing up to 141% increase in throughput.

fast, so that it effectively writes pagewise, which was not the target
of the test.

Thanks,
Horst

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing Horst's concern about performance regressions when using large folios and strictlimiting off, explaining that a commit (494d2f508883) fixed the issue by changing writeback accounting. The author also provides additional benchmark results to support their claim.

Author asked for clarification on reviewer's test environment and results, specifically whether the issue was seen with strictlimiting enabled or disabled, and what test and server were used.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Horst,

Those results are with strictlimiting on. After commit 494d2f508883
('fuse: use default writeback accounting') [1], I didn't see any
performance regressions anymore with large folios + strictlimiting on.
More information on why that commit fixed the issue is in [2].

When I ran the benchmarks last week with strictlimiting off, I saw roughly:
    tmpfs:
      seq,  128k    1174 -> 1648 MiB/s    +40%
      seq,  1M      1261 -> 1845 MiB/s    +46%
      rand, 128k    1148 -> 1638 MiB/s    +43%
      rand, 1M      1273 -> 2065 MiB/s    +62%

    xfs on NVMe:
      seq,  128k     621 ->  740 MiB/s    +19%
      seq,  1M       649 ->  776 MiB/s    +20%
      rand, 128k    1020 -> 1515 MiB/s    +49%
      rand, 1M      1125 -> 1895 MiB/s    +68%

Strict limiting on actually had better performance here, which I think
is because with the small dirty limit, the dirtying and the writeback
happen in parallel instead of more dirty pages accumulating and then
writeback getting kicked off. Because the backing device is so fast,
it didn't cost throughput for the dirtying and the writeback to happen
concurrently. The benchmarks were run with fsync, so everything had to
be flushed before the fio run returned. If the backing device was slow
and writes were bursty and fsync wasn't enforced, I think there'd
probably be better performance with strictlimiting off than on, since
the writer would be throttled to the speed of the backing device with
strictlimtiing on.

---

Are you seeing this on your system with strictlimiting on or off? Is
that with commit 494d2f508883 in your tree? What test and server were
you running? Do you know what the speed of the backing device is?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/commit/fs/fuse/file.c?id=494d2f508883a6e5c4530e5c6b3c8b2bbfb7318d
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/CAJnrk1ZSaNRr-HWw-hbo2=LmbZiNGZveb0MwxZbPtBDFgg2icQ@mail.gmail.com/

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] iomap: add helpers to mark and clear folio uptodate state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, `iomap_folio_mark_uptodate` and `iomap_folio_clear_uptodate`, which allow filesystems to mark or clear the uptodate state of a folio and synchronize the iomap-internal uptodate bitmap. This is necessary for filesystems like fuse that need to update the uptodate state outside of conventional iomap calls, ensuring consistency between the folio's uptodate state and the iomap bitmap. The functions are exported as GPL symbols, making them available for use by other kernel components.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] fuse: use iomap helpers to update folio uptodate state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the FUSE filesystem to use iomap helpers for updating folio uptodate state, addressing a potential issue where the folio uptodate flag is cleared prematurely. The change affects how FUSE handles large folios backed by an internal bitmap.

review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jun 24, 2026 at 04:29:54AM +0100, Matthew Wilcox wrote:
> I believe this to be a bug.  I talked about this a bit as LSFMM, but the
> fundamental problem is that the folio uptodate flag DOES NOT BELONG TO THE
> FILESYSTEM (unlike the buffer_head uptodate flag, which definitely does).
> 
> The MM has a number of sanity checks that make sure we haven't
> done something awful like mapping a !uptodate folio into userspace.
> That would potentially leak confidential information to userspace.
> So when a filesystem clears the uptodate flag, the MM can come along
> and bring down the whole machine because the mapped folio is now !uptodate.
> 
> I know we have a terrible writeback error handling story.  But I think
> the right thing to do is just take out the calls to clear uptodate.
> In all filesystems.  Darrick took it out of iomap in e9c3a8e820ed but
> it's time to purge it everywhere.

Yeah.  We're not going to fix everything at once, but we should
slowly move to stop clearing the folio uptodate bit.  And I suspect
the same is trye for the sub-folio uptodate bits.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock, which allows each page counter to have its own cache of pre-charged pages. This addresses issues with the current implementation, including random eviction of memcgs when there are more than 7 competing for CPU resources, and tight coupling between stock and memcg. The new design enables separate stock caches for other page counters and simplifies charging logic. Stock usage and refills now happen via lockless atomic operations, eliminating the need for asynchronous workqueues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] mm/memcontrol: flatten try_charge_memcg control flow
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the try_charge_memcg function in mm/memcontrol.c to simplify its control flow by separating the logic for memsw and memory page counters into distinct paths. The change is made to improve readability, but does not introduce any functional changes. With this flattening, it will be easier to follow the control flow when page_counter_try_charge becomes stock-aware.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] mm/page_counter: introduce page_counter_try_charge_stock()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, page_counter_try_charge_stock(), which is a variant of the existing page_counter_try_charge() function. It first attempts to charge from per-cpu stock, then tries to refill the stock by overcharging up to the batch size if necessary, and finally charges exactly the requested number of pages if all else fails. The function reports back the number of pages charged to the caller, allowing for more efficient handling of memory limits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the memory control (memcg) system to use page_counter_stock instead of its own stock handling logic. This change removes special casing for spinning and refilling, allows for failure during allocation if there's not enough memory, and updates drain_all_stock and memcg_hotplug_cpu_dead to use the new stock drain variant. As a result, memsw and memory page counters now have independent stocks, which can lead to transient discrepancies in reported usage.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that page_counter_try_charge_stock() can succeed by consuming memsw stock and charging 0 new pages, but the memory-failure path unconditionally uncharges nr_pages from memsw, leading to a real memsw usage decrement even in case of failed allocation. He requested a transactional rollback.
review_comments.raw_body:
This needs a transactional rollback. page_counter_try_charge_stock() can
succeed by consuming memsw stock and charging 0 new pages, but the
memory-failure path unconditionally uncharges nr_pages from memsw.
That turns a failed allocation into a real memsw usage decrement.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unused code related to handling 'memcg_stock' in the Linux kernel's memory control (mm/memcontrol) module. The patch preserves the 'FLUSHING_CACHED_CHARGE' flag, which is still needed by another part of the code. The changes involve deleting 186 lines of code from a single file (mm/memcontrol.c).

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from struct mem_cgroup to page_counter, reducing memory footprint and eliminating locking and synchronous work_on_cpu. This change simplifies memcontrol code and enables the addition of a new page_counter to track toptier usage. However, it introduces a potential increase in memory overcharging on large machines with many cgroups.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: mm/hwpoison: persist poisoned PFN list across kexec via KHO [RFC]
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where poisoned PFN (Page Frame Number) lists are not persisted across kexec, which can lead to memory failures in the new kernel. The issue arises because some memory allocations occur before buddy is initialized and can land on bad frames. To solve this, the patch proposes using EFI_UNUSABLE_MEMORY to mark non-usable memory as nomap, making it invisible to both allocators and KASLR. This approach ensures that the bad frame is not accessible in the new kernel.

review_comments.author: Pratyush Yadav
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Pratyush Yadav suggested denying kexec if any pages in the scratch memory area are poisoned, as this would prevent unreliable booting; he also noted that an early check for poisoned pages is necessary due to recent changes
review_comments.raw_body:
With KHO, you have "scratch memory", a pre-reserved area of memory on
cold boot. The kernel image is always in this area when KHO is used. I
think it would be a fair idea to deny kexec if any of the pages in this
scratch area are poisoned. Because at that point you can't reliably boot
anyway.

Normally, all allocations between setup_arch() and memblock_free_all()
_also_ happen from scratch memory, so this check would solve the first
problem too... but I recently added patches [0] to change this. So I
think we do need to identify the poisoned pages early in boot.

[0] https://lore.kernel.org/kexec/20260605183501.3884950-16-pratyush@kernel.org/

[...]

-- 
Regards,
Pratyush Yadav

Reviewer Breno Leitao agreed that the patch's wording was incorrect and that running memory_failure() against an already-allocated frame is not ideal, but still better than the current situation.

Reviewer suggested using EFI_UNUSABLE_MEMORY to mark poisoned pages instead of KHO, allowing kexec to handle the issue without needing KHO.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: replace writeback inhibition xarray with a fixed inline buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: replace writeback inhibition xarray with a fixed inline buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the xarray used to track inhibited extent buffers in Btrfs transactions with a fixed inline buffer, managed by a CLOCK eviction policy. This change addresses two issues: a write_iops regression caused by frequent xarray stores during COW operations and an unbounded cleanup walk due to long iteration over inhibited buffers. The new approach uses a small fixed-size array to track the working set of buffers revisited across search_again descents, with a CLOCK eviction policy that ensures frequently accessed buffers are retained while less frequently accessed ones are reclaimed first.

review_comments.author: Sun YangKai
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that the design is clean but suggested non-blocking improvements

Reviewer Sun YangKai suggested using an overflowing add operation with masking to replace the modulo operator when indexing into the fixed inline buffer, and questioned the usefulness of storing additional information in the higher bits.

The reviewer suggested extracting the inhibit preparation logic into a helper function, likely to improve code organization and readability.

Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that using u8 for inhibited_ebs fields is a straightforward choice, even if the limit is later extended to 16 slots, and only the bitmap field would need to be widened to u16.
sentiment_signals: no clear disagreement or request for change
review_comments.raw_body:
Reviewed-by: Sun YangKai <sunk67188@gmail.com>

Thanks for your great work. The overall direction looks nice and the 
design is clean. I've got some non\u2011blocking suggestions, though.

---

I think we can using overflowing add with ` & (SLOTS - 1)` as the index 
as long as SLOTS is power of 2, instead of `% SLOTS`. And the higher 
bits can parsed as number of "rounds" if we keep them, and I'm not sure 
if the extra info is useful for any purpose.

---

And personally I'd love to wrap this part of logic into a helper 
function like

static inline u8 btrfs_inhibit_prepare_slot(struct btrfs_trans_handle 
*trans) {...}

Or something with a better name.

---

Given the 8-slot limit for inhibited_ebs (4 bits (8) for capacity, 8 
bits for bitmap), using u8 for these two fields is a clear and 
straightforward choice IMO. Even if we later extend the limit to 16, 
only the bitmap field (inhibited_ebs_referenced) would need to be 
widened to u16.

Thanks,
Sun YangKai

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 1/7] mm, swap: add virtual swap device infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces the virtual swap device infrastructure, which creates a massive virtual swap device at boot using dynamic cluster allocation. The infrastructure includes per-cluster dynamic information and a side table for vswap backend metadata. The patch is part of a larger series that will enable hot paths to skip per-device bookkeeping and allow backends such as zswap and zero to interact with the vswap layer. The virtual swap device cannot be swapon'd or swapoff'd yet, but this patch lays the groundwork for subsequent patches to implement these features.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 2/7] mm, swap: support zswap and zeroswap as vswap backends
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zswap and zeroswap as virtual swap backends, allowing pages to be compressed or detected as zero-filled without pre-allocating physical swap slots. It builds on top of the existing swap-table infrastructure and introduces virtual swap entries that decouple PTE swap entries from physical backing. The patch only supports zswap and zero-page backends for now, with physical disk backing fallback and writeback support to be added in subsequent patches.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 3/7] mm, swap: support physical swap as a vswap backend
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for physical swap as a backend for the virtual swap layer (vswap). It allows vswap to allocate a physical slot on demand when needed, serving as a fallback for zswap_store failures or as the destination for zswap writeback. The physical slot is tracked via a Pointer-tagged swap_table entry on the physical cluster, referencing the vswap entry. This patch modifies various kernel files, including mm/memory.c, mm/page_io.c, and mm/vmscan.c, to enable this functionality.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 4/7] mm, swap: only charge physical swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the way memory cgroups (memcgs) charge swap usage to only count physical swap entries, rather than virtual ones. This change is made by deferring the memcg->swap charge for vswap entries until physical backing is allocated. The patch splits the lifecycle of vswap into four operations: recording the memcg private ID at allocation without charging, charging memcg->swap when physical backing is allocated, uncharging in __vswap_release_backing, and dropping the ID ref at __swap_cluster_free_entries. This change aims to make memcg->swap reflect actual on-disk swap usage rather than virtual swap reservations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 5/7] mm, swap: add debugfs counters for vswap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two debugfs counters to track the usage of virtual swap slots in Linux. The 'used' counter keeps track of the number of virtual swap slots currently allocated, while the 'alloc_reject' counter counts the cumulative number of failed vswap allocations. This allows developers and administrators to monitor the performance and behavior of virtual swap on their system.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 6/7] mm, swap: defer memcg_table allocation on physical clusters
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory allocation for physical swap clusters by deferring the allocation of memcg_table until it's actually needed. In a CONFIG_VSWAP build, memcg_table is allocated eagerly only when the cluster contains vswap allocations or direct-use slots. Otherwise, the allocation is deferred to alloc_swap_scan_cluster, which lazy-allocates on the first direct-use slot and skips entirely for clusters holding Pointer-tagged vswap backings. This change reduces memory waste on vswap-heavy workloads where zswap writeback is the only consumer of physical swap.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 7/7] mm, swap: widen swap_info_struct max/pages to unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch widens the size of two fields in the swap_info_struct structure from unsigned int to unsigned long, specifically max and pages. This change allows the vswap device to exceed its current 16 TB capacity limit by accommodating larger cluster sizes up to approximately 8 PB. The update does not affect physical swap devices, which continue to be bounded independently of these changes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), which aims to address the limitations of current swap design by decoupling swap entries from physical backing slots. This allows for more efficient use of disk space, especially in scenarios where zswap is used. The changes include rebasing to a newer mm-unstable tip, fixing assorted issues, and simplifying code. Additionally, the patch series introduces new features such as deferring memcg_table allocation on physical clusters until the first vswap-backing slot installs and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.

Tier 1: VSWAP, Tier 2: ZSWAP ...

I don't see any problem applying the desired functionality with the
currently proposed mechanism and interface. With this, a user would be
assigned the default Virtual -> RAM swap tier, and the overall picture
becomes one where swap tiers are composed according to the priority
setting.

A few more thoughts came to mind.

Shakeel also proposed a per-tier max for the swap tier interface.

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/aiw2p5ANjsQUCIHA@linux.dev/

amount via such a per-tier max, I think the current interface is the
better fit. (But, as Shakeel mentioned, if we only allow the limit
to be set to 0 or max, the usage could end up being the same. I'm still
thinking this part through.)

I have a few other thoughts as well, but I plan to raise those points in
the swap tier discussion thread instead. Please take a look at the
related thread, and let me know if you have any opinions. :)

And I'll share more if other thoughts come to mind

Thanks,
Youngjun Park

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] iova: convert from rbtree to maple tree
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the hand-rolled red-black tree in the IOVA allocator with a Maple tree, a B-tree variant designed for range tracking and gap searching. The change reduces memory usage by shrinking the size of struct iova from 40 bytes to 16 bytes and improves search performance by using O(log_10 n) search depth and better cache locality. The patch also simplifies the code by eliminating the need for retry loops and successor gap fixups, and introduces a new allocation strategy that over-requests memory to guarantee alignment. Overall, this patch aims to improve both memory efficiency and search performance in the IOVA allocator.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] iova: add KUnit test suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a KUnit test suite for the maple-tree-based IOVA allocator, which is responsible for managing I/O virtual address (IOVA) ranges. The test suite includes various test cases to verify the correctness of the allocator's behavior under different scenarios, such as alignment, preference, reservation, and stress testing. The tests are designed to ensure that the allocator correctly handles allocation and deallocation of IOVAs, and that it maintains invariants such as non-overlapping entries and correct pfn_lo/pfn_hi values. The test suite is compiled only when the test configuration is enabled.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a mechanism to defer erasing entries from the maple tree when GFP_ATOMIC fails, which can occur under memory pressure. When an erase attempt fails, the entry is marked in-place with a special marker (IOVA_DEFERRED) and the struct iova is freed immediately. The deferred erase is retried by subsequent allocations or frees in the domain via a bounding range. This approach keeps the size of struct iova at 16 bytes and moves the rare-path state to the maple tree, eliminating the need for timers or workqueues. The patch also includes test cases to exercise this new behavior.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the iova (Input/Output Virtual Address) allocation code to use a maple tree data structure, which improves performance and reduces the likelihood of soft lockups caused by linear search in alloc_iova(). The patches also add self-tests for the iova code and reduce memory usage. The changes were tested on both virtual machines and physical systems with IOMMU enabled.
